stories.headline: Britain, 40+ Nations Demand Iran Reopen Strait of Hormuz as Trump Issues Expletive-Laden Threat
stories.summary:
Britain has accused Iran of holding the global economy hostage as diplomats from more than 40 countries convened to press Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the critical shipping lane through which a significant portion of the world's oil supply passes. The diplomatic gathering comes as the U.S.-Iran conflict continues to escalate, with Iran maintaining its blockade despite mounting international pressure and direct U.S. military action.

President Trump intensified his rhetoric on Saturday in a profanity-laden post on Truth Social, warning Iran that 'Tuesday will be Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day, all wrapped up in one' if the Strait is not reopened by his Monday deadline. The post injected fresh volatility into the conflict hours after U.S. commandos completed a high-risk rescue mission to extract an injured American airman from inside Iranian territory.

Iran has responded defiantly, threatening to open 'the gates of hell' if the United States follows through on Trump's threatened strikes against power plants and bridges. Tehran's position has drawn concern from allied nations who warn that attacks on civilian infrastructure could further destabilize the region and trigger a broader humanitarian crisis.

The standoff over the Strait of Hormuz has sent shockwaves through global energy markets, with analysts warning that a prolonged closure could sharply drive up oil prices and contribute to inflation worldwide. The IMF has separately cautioned that the conflict is steering the global economy toward 'higher prices and slower growth,' increasing pressure on both sides to find a diplomatic off-ramp.

stories.headline: Hungary Says Explosives Found Near Gas Pipeline Carrying Russian Supply, Alleges Foreign Plot Ahead of Election
stories.summary:
Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban announced Saturday that authorities discovered explosives near a gas pipeline in Serbia that carries Russian natural gas into Hungary, alleging a plot to blow up the critical energy infrastructure. The announcement came just days before a pivotal Hungarian national election, raising immediate questions about the timing of the disclosure.

Officials in Budapest did not immediately identify a suspect or nation responsible for the alleged plot, but the accusation fits a pattern of Orban's government framing foreign interference as a threat to Hungary's energy security and political sovereignty. The pipeline in question is a key conduit for Russian gas supplies to Central Europe at a time when the broader region has been working to reduce its dependence on Russian energy.

The allegation follows a series of infrastructure attacks across Europe in recent years, including drone strikes on Russia's Baltic port of Ust-Luga, which resumed crude oil loading over the weekend after days of disruptions caused by Ukrainian drone attacks. European security officials have been on heightened alert for sabotage of energy infrastructure since the 2022 Nord Stream pipeline explosions.

Critics of Orban's government, including opposition parties, have questioned whether the announcement is being used as a political tool ahead of the election, a charge the government has denied. If confirmed, an attack on the pipeline would represent a significant escalation targeting energy supply routes in a NATO member state.

stories.headline: Iran Detains Family Members of Exiled Activists as War Intensifies Domestic Crackdown
stories.summary:
Iran's government is systematically detaining relatives of opposition figures living abroad, using family members as leverage to silence exiled activists critical of the Islamic Republic, according to multiple accounts gathered by the Associated Press. Intelligence agents have taken siblings, nieces, and mothers of dissidents into custody, with families reporting they are held incommunicado at facilities run by Iran's Intelligence Ministry.

Among those affected is Hossein Razzagh, a former political prisoner now in Europe, whose politically uninvolved brother Ali was detained in Tehran on March 15. Ali was able to make a brief phone call to his wife from a detention center before contact was cut off entirely. Activist Sareh Sedighi, whose 2021 death sentence was overturned before she fled Iran, said her mother was taken from her home last month, despite suffering from health problems requiring daily insulin treatment.

The crackdown has been compounded by an internet blackout imposed during earlier nationwide protests, which human rights watchdogs say resulted in security forces killing thousands of demonstrators. Iran's judiciary has also begun seizing property belonging to exiled activists, adding economic pressure to the threat of harm to relatives.

The detentions represent a documented escalation in Iran's tactics against the diaspora opposition community at a moment when the country is engaged in an active military conflict with the United States and Israel. Activists say the pressure is intended to force them to cease speaking with foreign media and tracking abuses inside the country.

stories.headline: Two Pennsylvania Fire Chiefs Killed in Head-On Crash While Searching for Missing Woman
stories.summary:
Two senior members of the Walnuttown Fire Company in Berks County, Pennsylvania were killed Saturday evening when their utility vehicle was struck head-on by a Toyota Camry on Route 222, roughly 45 miles northwest of Philadelphia. Fire Chief Jeff Buck and Assistant Fire Chief Robert Shick, Jr., both residents of Fleetwood, died at the scene of the crash, according to the Berks County Coroner.

The pair were traveling north in the utility vehicle along the shoulder of Route 222 as part of a search for a missing woman when the southbound Camry swerved off the road and struck them. Video from the scene showed the utility vehicle on its side. A male and a female occupant of the Camry fled the scene following the crash but were subsequently arrested by police.

No additional details about the arrests or the ongoing search for the missing woman were immediately available. Autopsies for both fire chiefs were scheduled for Monday.

The Walnuttown Fire Company acknowledged the loss in a Facebook post, writing: "At this time we would like to send our thoughts and prayers" to the families of Buck and Shick, adding, "Rest easy chiefs, we got it from here."

stories.headline: Trump Eyes New Attorney General as Cabinet Reshuffling Signals Shift in Administration's Second Phase
stories.summary:
President Trump is weighing replacements for Attorney General Pam Bondi following her firing, with Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche — now serving as acting attorney general — and EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin emerging as leading candidates. Bondi's dismissal came weeks after the ouster of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, marking the first two Cabinet departures of Trump's second term and raising questions about the administration's direction.

Blanche, who previously served as Trump's personal attorney and has been the public face of the Justice Department on immigration enforcement and the release of Jeffrey Epstein-related files, is seen as a natural successor. Zeldin, a former congressman and Army veteran with a legal background, has been described by Trump as the administration's 'secret weapon.' Republicans are said to be pushing for a swift confirmation process for whoever is nominated.

The cabinet shake-ups represent a potential departure from what former adviser Steve Bannon described as a 'no scalps policy' in Trump's second term — a deliberate effort to project stability in contrast to the frequent turnover of his first administration. Critics, including congressional Democrats, argue the firings reflect an effort to install officials more willing to pursue politically motivated prosecutions against Trump's opponents.

The next attorney general will inherit a Justice Department navigating wartime legal pressures, ongoing immigration litigation, and politically sensitive investigations. The selection is expected to have significant consequences for the department's independence and its handling of cases involving Trump's political rivals.

stories.headline: AI Transforms Bollywood Production, Slashing Costs and Timelines as Hollywood Lags Behind
stories.summary:
Artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping India's film industry — the world's most prolific by output — enabling studios to significantly reduce production timelines and costs, according to industry figures. The technology is being deployed across casting, visual effects, dubbing, and script development, accelerating a sector that produces thousands of films annually across dozens of languages.

The adoption in India stands in contrast to Hollywood, where union contracts and widespread fear of job displacement have led studios to proceed cautiously with AI integration. Indian productions have faced fewer institutional barriers, allowing faster experimentation and implementation across major and regional studios alike.

Proponents argue AI allows smaller productions to compete with larger studios on production quality and speed, potentially democratizing filmmaking across India's diverse regional markets. Critics and labor advocates warn that the shift risks displacing translators, background artists, voice actors, and other below-the-line workers who have limited formal protections compared to their counterparts in the United States or Europe.

The divergence between India and Hollywood over AI adoption is being closely watched by the global entertainment industry as a case study in how different regulatory and labor environments shape technological change. The long-term effects on employment and creative quality in both markets remain subjects of debate.

stories.headline: Congress Divided Over Trump's $1.5 Trillion Defense Budget Request as Iran War Continues
stories.summary:
Members of Congress from both parties are expressing skepticism about President Trump's 2027 budget request, which calls for $1.5 trillion in Defense Department funding — a 44 percent increase over the current year's appropriation. The request also includes a 10 percent reduction in non-defense spending, amounting to a $73 billion cut to domestic programs, along with funding for Trump's proposed Golden Dome missile defense system and replenishment of military munitions as the U.S.-Iran war enters its sixth week.

Sen. Tim Kaine, D-Va., a member of the Senate Armed Services Committee, told NBC News' "Meet the Press" that he would have "a hard time seeing that size of an increase as being justified." Kaine also questioned the Pentagon's ability to manage the funds given Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's recent decision to fire several senior Army commanders during active combat operations, calling those dismissals a sign of "inept" civilian leadership. He also characterized the ongoing conflict with Iran as "illegal and unwise" and called on Congress to vote on a formal declaration of war.

Rep. Mike Lawler, R-N.Y., also appearing on the program, acknowledged that the administration's budget request would not be adopted as submitted, saying Congress would "continue to ensure that our armed forces have the resources that they need." Lawler pushed back on Kaine's description of the war as illegal, and suggested that potential use of ground troops should be discussed with Congress in a classified setting, with the retrieval of enriched uranium cited as the only scenario he could envision warranting such action.

The budget debate comes as the administration simultaneously pursues large-scale military operations and deep cuts to civilian agencies, a combination that is drawing scrutiny from lawmakers on both sides of the aisle. Congressional hearings on the budget request are expected in the coming weeks.

stories.headline: Trump Says U.S. Commandos Rescued Downed Airman from Inside Iran; Threatens to Strike Power Plants
stories.summary:
President Trump announced that U.S. special operations forces successfully extracted an injured Air Force crew member from inside Iran, completing a high-risk mission to recover the airman who had been missing since his F-15E was shot down last week. Trump hailed the rescue as a significant military achievement as the conflict, now in its sixth week, has resulted in the loss of multiple American aircraft and escalating strikes across the region.

Following the announcement, Trump issued a fresh threat to target Iranian power plants if Tehran does not allow the Strait of Hormuz to reopen to commercial shipping. The strait, a critical chokepoint for global energy supplies, has been a central flashpoint in the conflict. Trump also told Fox News that Iran was engaged in negotiations and that a deal could be possible by Monday, a characterization that has caused confusion among allies and financial markets given contradictory signals from the administration.

The rescue operation marks a rare piece of positive news for the administration after days of intensifying combat, including reports of additional U.S. aircraft being hit and drone and missile strikes reaching Kuwait, Bahrain, and Lebanon. The fate of other missing or captured personnel has not been fully disclosed.

The mixed signals from Washington — simultaneously threatening escalation while suggesting diplomatic progress is imminent — have been noted by both supporters and critics as emblematic of the broader uncertainty surrounding U.S. strategy in the conflict. Markets and allied governments have been closely watching for any clearer indication of American objectives and timeline.

stories.headline: Vehicle Strikes Revelers at Lao New Year Parade in Rural Louisiana, Injuring About 15
stories.summary:
A vehicle struck a crowd of revelers at a Lao New Year parade Saturday afternoon in the Lanxang Village area near New Iberia, Louisiana, injuring approximately 15 people, some seriously. Two patients were airlifted by helicopter, and 10 ambulances responded to the scene. The driver, a 57-year-old man from Jeanerette, was quickly arrested at the scene.

Authorities said the driver appeared impaired upon arrival and later tested positive for a high blood alcohol level. He was charged with impaired, negligent, and careless driving, as well as having an open container of alcohol in the vehicle. The Iberia Parish Sheriff's Office said preliminary investigation indicates the incident does not appear to have been intentional.

The annual celebration, held over three days on the grounds of Wat Thammarattanaram Buddhist temple, draws thousands of attendees and features Southeast Asian food, live music, and a parade. Afternoon and evening events were canceled following the incident, though organizers said religious services would proceed on Sunday.

Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry issued a statement saying he and his wife were praying for those affected and expressed gratitude to first responders. Festival organizers said on Facebook they were "profoundly saddened" by the incident and were praying for the victims and their families.

stories.headline: Gunfire Reported Near Lafayette Park Across from White House; No Injuries, No Suspect Found
stories.summary:
The U.S. Secret Service said Sunday it is investigating reports of overnight gunfire near Lafayette Park, located directly across the street from the White House in Washington, D.C. No injuries were reported following the incident, which occurred after midnight.

Secret Service agents searched Lafayette Park and the surrounding area but did not locate a suspect. The agency said it is working jointly with the District of Columbia Metropolitan Police and U.S. Park Police on the investigation.

President Donald Trump was spending the weekend at the White House at the time of the incident. White House operations continued as normal, though the Secret Service said security in the area was increased as a precaution. The park has been fenced off in recent weeks due to ongoing renovation work.

stories.headline: Trump Administration Moves to Build National Voter Database, Drawing Praise and Legal Challenges
stories.summary:
The Trump administration is intensifying its push to construct a national voter database, with President Trump signing an executive order this week directing the consolidation of citizenship and voter registration data. The Justice Department has finalized an agreement with the Department of Homeland Security to share sensitive voter-roll information obtained from states, to be checked against a federal citizenship verification system critics say is prone to inaccuracies. The administration has now sued 30 states and the District of Columbia for refusing to voluntarily hand over unredacted voter lists.

The latest escalation includes a newly deputized federal prosecutor — Dan Bishop, the U.S. attorney for the Middle District of North Carolina, who previously questioned the outcome of the 2020 election — tasked with examining voter registration data and hunting for suspected noncitizen voters. Officials also floated the idea of conditioning hundreds of millions of dollars in homeland security grants on states sharing voter data with federal immigration records systems, though that policy had not been formally implemented as of Friday.

Supporters of the effort, including the White House, describe it as a necessary step to protect election integrity and ensure that only eligible citizens are casting ballots. Critics, including election law experts and Democratic officials, argue the effort unconstitutionally shifts election administration from states to the federal government and could be weaponized to purge eligible voters from rolls ahead of the 2026 midterm elections.

The executive order came the same week Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi, raising additional questions about the direction of the Justice Department's election enforcement activities. The constitutional authority of the federal government to compel states to surrender voter data remains the central legal question being fought in dozens of federal courtrooms across the country.

stories.headline: Iran-U.S. Conflict Widens: Missile Strikes Hit Gulf States as Search Continues for Missing American Airman
stories.summary:
Iran launched fresh missile and drone strikes Saturday against Gulf states, Iraq, and Israel, escalating a conflict now in its sixth week. Kuwait reported that Iranian drone attacks knocked out two power generation stations and struck water desalination plants, while earlier strikes caused significant damage to a government office complex and set fire to Kuwait's oil ministry. Israel said it intercepted multiple missiles fired from Iran overnight.

The strikes came as both the United States and Iran conducted searches for a missing American weapons systems officer after a U.S. F-15E Strike Eagle was shot down over Iran on Friday. The aircraft's pilot was recovered, but the second crew member remained missing Saturday. Iranian state media reported that Iranian officials were urging citizens to help locate the missing American 'alive' and were offering bounties for the crew member's capture. A U.S. A-10 Warthog dispatched on a search-and-rescue mission was also hit and damaged by Iranian fire, though its pilot ejected safely over the Gulf.

President Trump reiterated his threat on Truth Social to resume strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure if Iran did not reopen the Strait of Hormuz or agree to a deal within 48 hours, warning that 'all Hell will reign down on them.' Iran's senior military commanders rejected the ultimatum outright, with Gen. Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi warning that 'the gates of hell will open for you' and describing Trump's statement as a 'helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid action.'

The U.S. and Israel continued their bombardment of Iranian military, energy, and industrial sites on Saturday. The widening of strikes to Gulf state infrastructure marks a significant escalation with potential consequences for global energy supplies and regional stability, as international diplomats continue efforts to broker a ceasefire.

stories.headline: Iran-U.S. War Escalates as Tehran Fires Missiles at Gulf States, Israel; Trump Issues Fresh 48-Hour Ultimatum
stories.summary:
The conflict between the United States and Iran intensified Saturday as Iran launched additional missile barrages targeting Gulf states including Kuwait, Bahrain, and the UAE, as well as Israel. Kuwait reported that Iranian drone strikes knocked out two power generation stations and damaged water desalination plants, with earlier attacks also striking a government office complex and sparking a fire at the Kuwaiti oil ministry. The U.S. and Israel continued their bombardment of Iranian military, energy, and industrial sites.

President Donald Trump renewed his threat on Truth Social, warning that 'all Hell will reign down on' Iran if it did not reopen the Strait of Hormuz or agree to a deal within 48 hours. Iranian officials rejected the ultimatum outright, with Gen. Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi of Iran's central military command calling Trump's statement 'helpless, nervous, unbalanced and stupid' and warning that 'the gates of hell will open for you' if hostilities escalated further.

The standoff continued against the backdrop of a search for a missing American weapons systems officer after a U.S. F-15E was shot down over Iran on Friday. U.S. media reported the pilot was rescued, while Iranian state media said at least four people were killed in a U.S.-Israeli airstrike in the same region. Unverified social media videos showed Iranians converging on a mountainous area of southwestern Iran to search for the missing American crew member, with Iranian officials offering bounties for capture.

The broadening conflict has drawn in multiple regional actors and raised alarm among humanitarian organizations, who warn that disruptions to the Strait of Hormuz and key logistics hubs in Dubai and Doha are severely hampering global aid delivery. The United Nations has described the supply chain disruption as the most significant since COVID-19, with shipping costs rising as much as 20 percent and deliveries rerouted around Africa, adding weeks to transit times.

stories.headline: Iran War Disrupts Global Humanitarian Aid Supply Chains, Relief Groups Warn
stories.summary:
Major international aid organizations are warning that the U.S.-Iran war and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz have severely disrupted global humanitarian supply chains, threatening food and medicine delivery to millions of vulnerable people worldwide. The World Food Program says tens of thousands of metric tons of food are heavily delayed in transit, while the International Rescue Committee reports $130,000 worth of pharmaceuticals for Sudan stranded in Dubai and nearly 670 boxes of therapeutic food for malnourished children in Somalia stuck in India.

Key shipping routes through the Strait of Hormuz and logistics hubs in Dubai, Doha, and Abu Dhabi have been significantly impacted, forcing aid groups to reroute vessels around Africa or use expensive hybrid land-sea-air transport options. The United Nations has characterized the disruption as the most significant to global supply chains since COVID-19, with costs rising up to 20 percent and delivery timelines stretching by weeks or more.

UNICEF reported it is now flying vaccines to Turkey and driving them into Iran rather than shipping directly, sharply increasing costs for vaccination campaigns. Aid officials cautioned that even after fighting stops, the shock to supply chains could continue delaying lifesaving assistance for months.

The crisis compounds existing strains on international aid budgets, which had already been severely weakened by steep U.S. cuts to foreign assistance. Relief organizations are urging an immediate ceasefire and restoration of shipping routes, warning that the confluence of funding cuts and supply chain collapse risks pushing humanitarian operations 'beyond their limits,' according to the International Rescue Committee.

stories.headline: DHS and Democratic Senator Clash Over ICE Arrest of Honduran Man in Baltimore
stories.summary:
The Department of Homeland Security and Sen. Chris Van Hollen, D-Md., offered sharply conflicting accounts Saturday of an Immigration and Customs Enforcement arrest incident in Baltimore that left a man hospitalized. DHS identified the individual as Ever Omar Alvarenga-Rios, a Honduran national with a final order of removal dating to 2018, and said he recklessly evaded officers, caused a multi-vehicle crash, and sent two ICE agents to the hospital. The agency said officers used the minimum force necessary to complete the arrest.

Van Hollen posted photographs of the man in a hospital bed on social media, describing him as an 'asylum seeker' who was rear-ended by an ICE vehicle while driving to work and who suffered significant injuries to his head, chest, back, and hands. The senator also alleged ICE was denying the man access to attorneys, which DHS did not directly address in its public statement.

DHS Acting Assistant Secretary Lauren Bis said in a statement that the man 'broke our laws, resisted arrest, sent two ICE law enforcement officers to the hospital, and endangered the general public,' adding that both officers are expected to make full recoveries. DHS also used the incident to criticize what it called 'sanctuary politicians' who it said encourage illegal immigrants to evade arrest through informational webinars.

The dispute reflects a broader national debate over the conduct and scope of immigration enforcement operations. Immigrant rights advocates argue that aggressive enforcement tactics risk harming individuals with legitimate legal claims, while administration supporters contend that enforcement of standing removal orders is both lawful and necessary for public safety.

stories.headline: Iran War Escalates: Drone and Missile Strikes Hit Kuwait, Bahrain, and Lebanon as A-10 Aircraft Reported Hit
stories.summary:
The U.S.-Iran conflict continued to expand Saturday, with drone attacks causing significant damage to Kuwait's Finance Ministry complex and striking energy storage facilities in Bahrain. Kuwait's Electricity Ministry reported that two power generation units were taken out of service following Sunday's attack, while Bapco Energies said a fire at its storage facilities was extinguished with no injuries reported. Iran's state media separately claimed an A-10 aircraft was hit by Iranian fire, adding to earlier reports of U.S. aircraft losses in the conflict.

Israeli airstrikes continued in Lebanon, striking residential areas in the southern port city of Tyre and the village of Saksakiyeh. Images from the region showed widespread destruction, with displaced Lebanese families sheltering in Beirut. Protests calling for an end to the war drew crowds in Tel Aviv, where residents also sought shelter in bomb shelters during Iranian missile strikes.

The conflict has begun to reverberate beyond the immediate theater. Bangladesh imposed energy austerity measures, cutting office hours and mall opening hours, as it seeks alternative fuel sources and external financing in response to disruptions caused by the war. The attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure signal an expanding front that threatens global oil and gas supplies.

Iranian judiciary media reported the arrest of two individuals, identified as Mohammad Ami Biglari and Shahin Vahedparast, as Iran continues to manage internal security amid the ongoing conflict. The war, now in its fifth week, has drawn in multiple regional actors and continues to escalate with no ceasefire in sight.

stories.headline: Trump's 2027 Budget Proposes 44% Defense Spending Increase, Deep Cuts to Domestic Programs
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump released his 2027 budget proposal Friday, requesting $1.5 trillion for the Pentagon — a 44% increase over current levels and the largest such request in decades. The plan, which Budget Director Russell Vought described as fulfilling Trump's promise to reinvest in national security, also calls for a 10% reduction in non-defense discretionary spending. The budget reflects the administration's wartime posture as the U.S.-led conflict with Iran enters its fifth week.

Among the proposed cuts are a 19% reduction in the Agriculture Department budget, a 13% cut for Housing and Urban Development, and roughly a 12% decrease for Health and Human Services, including elimination of a low-income home heating assistance program. The plan also cancels more than $15 billion in Biden-era infrastructure law spending, including renewable energy and NOAA grants, while ending certain university grant programs.

On the spending side, the proposal includes a $10 billion fund for construction and beautification projects in Washington, D.C., a $481 million increase in aviation safety and air traffic control hiring, and sustained funding for immigration enforcement operations including 100,000 adult detention beds and 30,000 family beds. Trump told a private White House event that wartime conditions make domestic social programs a lower federal priority, suggesting states should assume responsibility for programs like Medicaid and day care.

The president's annual budget is a statement of priorities and does not carry the force of law; Congress is free to reject it and frequently does. The proposal is expected to face significant resistance even within Republican ranks, particularly from members representing rural districts that rely on Agriculture Department funding and low-income energy assistance. Democrats have broadly criticized the proposed domestic cuts as harmful to working families.

stories.headline: Detroit Pistons Clinch No. 1 Eastern Conference Seed for First Time Since 2007
stories.summary:
The Detroit Pistons clinched the top seed in the Eastern Conference playoffs Saturday night, beating the Philadelphia 76ers 116-93. It marks the first time the franchise has held the East's top seed since the 2006-07 season, representing a dramatic turnaround for a team that endured years of rebuilding. The Pistons improved to 56-21 on the season with the victory.

Tobias Harris led Detroit with 19 points, while Daniss Jenkins contributed 16 points and 14 assists. Jalen Duren added 16 points and Ausar Thompson chipped in 14, with the Pistons building a commanding second-half lead after outscoring Philadelphia in the final minutes of the first half. The win is particularly notable as it came without star guard Cade Cunningham, who has missed 10 games with a collapsed left lung; Detroit is 8-2 in those contests.

For Philadelphia, Tyrese Maxey led all scorers with 23 points, and Paul George added 20 in his continued return from a 25-game drug policy suspension. The 76ers played without center Joel Embiid, who was held out with a right oblique injury. Philadelphia had won eight of its previous 11 games entering the matchup.

The Pistons' rise to the conference's top seed is one of the notable stories of the NBA season, coming after years of lottery finishes. Detroit will host home-court advantage throughout the Eastern Conference playoffs.

stories.headline: Federal Judge Halts White House Ballroom Project, Ruling Construction Requires Congressional Approval
stories.summary:
A federal judge this week ordered a halt to construction of President Trump's planned 90,000-square-foot White House ballroom, ruling that the president lacks the statutory authority to proceed without Congressional approval. Judge Leon, in a 35-page ruling, wrote that the president is "the steward of the White House for future generations of First Families" but "not the owner," adding that no statute grants the authority claimed by the administration. The Department of Justice has appealed the decision.

Despite the ruling, the National Capital Planning Commission proceeded with a vote Thursday to approve the project, with commission chair Will Scharf stating the judge's order only prevented construction activity rather than planning deliberations. The commission voted 8-1 in favor, with Republican Sen. Rand Paul of Kentucky casting a yes vote — drawing rare praise from Trump, who called Paul "an extraordinarily difficult vote" and publicly thanked him on Truth Social.

The ballroom, which is being funded by private donors rather than taxpayer funds, is part of what the White House calls an East Wing modernization project. Construction began after the East Wing was demolished in October 2025. The project is slated for completion by 2028, though the timeline is uncertain pending the outcome of the legal challenge.

The clash between the executive and judicial branches over the project reflects broader tensions during the Trump administration over the limits of presidential authority over federal property. Critics have argued the project bypasses required legislative oversight, while the administration maintains that private funding and presidential stewardship of the White House justify the construction.
